topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music swelled, a vibrant wave washing over the crowded ballroom. I spotted him across the room, leaning against a marble pillar, a familiar smirk already tugging at the corner of my mouth. He looked utterly unbothered, impossibly handsome in a tailored suit. My heart gave a little skip, a quickening rhythm against my ribs. I took a deep breath, adjusted the strap of my dress, and began a slow, deliberate circuit toward him, my steps light and bouncy. When I reached his side, I didn't stop; instead, I leaned in close, my voice dropping to a conspiratorial murmur, "Well, look what the cat dragged in. Still causing trouble, I see?" He turned, and the flicker of surprise in his eyes was exactly what I was hoping for. A wide grin spread across his face, and I knew I had exactly what I needed.

[story 2]

I was nursing a lukewarm champagne, observing the swirling chaos of the reception, when I caught sight of him near the dessert table. He was laughing at something a bridesmaid said, his head tilted back in that way that always made me feel a strange, warm jolt in my chest. A mischievous spark ignited behind my eyes. I decided inertia was no longer an option. I pushed off the wall and started moving, not with haste, but with a deliberate, meandering grace. I navigated the throng of well-wishers, my gaze locked on his. When I finally reached him, I stopped just close enough for him to catch the warmth radiating off me. "Fancy seeing you here," I said, my tone light, laced with an unmistakable teasing edge. I watched his expression shift—a slow unfolding of amusement—and I allowed myself a small, satisfied smile.

[story 3]

The air in the venue felt thick with celebration and expensive perfume. I was watching the dance floor, enjoying the sheer energy of the evening, when my peripheral vision snagged on him. He was talking animatedly to someone, his posture relaxed, radiating an easy confidence that always managed to catch my attention. A sudden, buoyant feeling bubbled up inside me, urging me toward him. I decided to make a direct approach. I walked over, my stride purposefully energetic, and paused directly in front of him, forcing him to acknowledge my presence. "Surprise," I announced, my voice carrying just enough volume to cut through the background noise. I watched his eyes meet mine, and I could see the shift—that familiar spark of recognition mixed with something else, something undeniably lighthearted, crossing his features.
